nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, typically reported at depths of 10–20 m

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, and other meaty foods, along with high-quality marine flake and marine pellet food. In the wild, tends to forage for small zooplankton and invertebrates near the reef.

avoidWith: other Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus species in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jokuyken15, 4658x3105. Sharp, vibrant male showing the diagnostic red dorsum, broad yellow flank stripe, and bluish-white belly; whole fish in frame, well-lit against a reef background.

summary: Naoko's Fairy Wrasse is a small, vividly colored reef fish from Indonesian waters, generally considered reef-safe and suitable for intermediate-level reef aquariums. A secure, tight-fitting lid is essential, as this species tends to jump.
careNotes: Naoko's Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus naokoae) was described by Randall & Tanaka in 2009, with the type specimen originating via the aquarium trade. It is known from the Eastern Indian Ocean around Sumatra, Indonesia, at depths of roughly 10–20 m, and adults typically reach 6–10 cm in length. Like most Cirrhilabrus fairy wrasses, this species has been reported to jump, and a tight-fitting canopy is strongly advised. Its semi-aggressive temperament primarily applies to conspecific males and other wrasses in the Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus genera; in mixed-species reef tanks with dissimilar fish, it tends to be more peaceful. Males display brighter coloration — red dorsally, bluish-white ventrally, with a broad yellow stripe — than females, and color intensity varies with mood. The IUCN lists this species as Vulnerable as of November 2023, reflecting its limited known range; wild-caught specimens should be sourced with conservation awareness in mind. Distinct pairing during spawning events has been documented in the wild, consistent with the harem-based social structure common to Cirrhilabrus wrasses, which are protogynous sequential hermaphrodites (meaning females can change sex to become males). For best results, keep as a single male or as a male with one or more females introduced simultaneously.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning has not been reliably reported for this species. In the wild, Naoko's Fairy Wrasse forms harem groups — one dominant male presiding over several females — and distinct male-female pairing has been observed during spawning events. Providing a similar social arrangement in a large reef system, with one male kept alongside multiple females, may encourage courtship display behavior, though successful breeding in home aquariums remains undocumented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page used for adult size (~4 in TL), temperature (72–78°F → 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), minimum tank (50 gal), care level, diet, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe status, and escape-risk note. The PRIMARY_SOURCE_URL supplied was the wrasse collection page; the direct product page was retrieved via search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria), maximum size (6.0 cm SL), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean: Indonesia, Sumatra), habitat depth (10–20 m), trophic level (3.3), distinct pairing during breeding. IUCN status: Vulnerable (VU). Size disagreement with LiveAquaria: FishBase 6.0 cm SL vs LiveAquaria ~10 cm TL — likely SL-vs-TL distinction; range of 6–10 cm adopted. FishBase ecology page was not in the public version, so water-parameter data came from LiveAquaria only.
